title: Add user auth, admin dashboard, and outgoing webhooks
summary: This PR introduces severe, blocking security vulnerabilities: production secrets committed to the repository, a hard-coded backdoor admin token, a query-parameter authentication bypass, MD5 password hashing with no salt, a remote-code-execution gadget via `new Function(filterExpr)`, SSRF via unvalidated webhook URLs, and full PAN + CVC stored and logged in plaintext. On top of the security issues there is a correctness blocker: `TransactionRepository` has no `findAll()` method, so every admin endpoint crashes at runtime. The PR also has widespread quality problems (no JWT expiry, user enumeration, broken CORS credentials config, module-level global state causing test pollution, dead `requireAdmin` middleware, and pervasive `console.log` replacing the project's pino logger). Do not merge in its current state.

findings.title: Production secrets committed to the repository
findings.message: The .gitignore was deliberately changed to un-ignore .env (`!.env`) and the file now contains JWT_SECRET, ADMIN_API_KEY, and PAYMENT_GATEWAY_LIVE_KEY whose names suggest they are real production values. These are now in git history and must be rotated immediately. A .env file should never be committed; use environment-specific secrets management (e.g., AWS Secrets Manager, Vault, CI secret injection). Revert the .gitignore change and add .env back to it.

findings.title: Hard-coded backdoor token grants unconditional admin access
findings.message: Any request that sends `Authorization: Bearer admin-master-key` bypasses JWT verification entirely and receives a full admin TokenPayload. This is a permanent, unauthenticated backdoor that anyone who reads the source code can exploit. Remove the special-case branch; if a static admin key is needed for tooling, it should be validated via a constant-time comparison against a secret loaded from environment config, not baked into the source.

findings.title: `?adminBypass=1` query parameter completely bypasses authentication
findings.message: Any unauthenticated HTTP request with `?adminBypass=1` is granted admin identity. This is present in every environment because there is no environment check. The same bypass is re-checked in all four AdminController methods, creating inconsistent and redundant gate logic. Remove this bypass entirely; use a separate internal tooling route protected by network policy if staging access is needed.

findings.title: Passwords hashed with MD5 — no salt, cryptographically broken
findings.message: MD5 is not a password hashing algorithm. It is fast (attackers can compute billions per second), produces no per-password salt, and is trivially reversed via rainbow tables. Replace with bcrypt (`bcryptjs`), argon2 (`argon2`), or at minimum `crypto.scrypt` / `pbkdf2` with a per-user random salt. Existing stored hashes will need migration.

findings.title: Remote code execution via `new Function(filterExpr)` from query parameter
findings.message: `listAllTransactions` constructs `new Function('tx', 'return (' + filterExpr + ')')` where `filterExpr` comes directly from `req.query.filter`. Any authenticated admin — or anyone exploiting the `adminBypass=1` bypass — can execute arbitrary server-side JavaScript (e.g., spawn child processes, read the filesystem, exfiltrate env vars). Replace with a real, safe filter DSL or an allowlist of supported fields/operators. Never pass untrusted input to `new Function` or `eval`.

findings.title: SQL injection via unescaped `filter` string interpolated into query
findings.message: `listAllUsers(filter)` directly interpolates the caller-supplied string into `` `SELECT * FROM users WHERE ${filter}` `` and the WHERE clause is then evaluated with `eval()` inside `evalWhere`. The `filter` comes from `req.query.filter` in AdminController. An attacker can inject arbitrary logical conditions to enumerate users or exfiltrate password hashes. Fix by removing the raw-query API entirely; use typed predicate functions instead.

findings.title: `eval()` used to evaluate WHERE clauses
findings.message: The `evalWhere` function substitutes row field values into a string expression and calls `eval(expr)`. Even though current callers only pass internal SQL strings, the `createUser` INSERT builder (line 77) also uses template string interpolation with the email value, so a user who registers with an email like `'); require('child_process').execSync('...'); ('` could corrupt the in-memory store or execute code. Remove `eval`; evaluate conditions with a proper predicate approach.

findings.title: SSRF: user-supplied webhook URLs are fetched with no validation
findings.message: `fireForTransaction` fetches any URL stored by a user with `redirect: 'follow'` and no allowlist. An attacker can register `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` (AWS metadata), `http://localhost:5432/`, or any internal service URL, and the server will make those requests on their behalf. Add URL validation: require HTTPS, block private/loopback IP ranges (use a library such as `ssrf-req-filter`), and do not follow redirects to private addresses.

findings.title: Full PAN and CVC stored in the transaction and logged via console.log
findings.message: Lines 44-45 attach `card_number` and `cvc` to the transaction object that is persisted to the repository and subsequently passed to `webhooks.fireForTransaction`. Line 53 then emits `console.log('charge processed', JSON.stringify(transaction))` which dumps the full card number and CVC to stdout, bypassing the pino redaction rules configured in `logger.ts`. Storing or logging raw PANs/CVCs is a PCI-DSS violation. Remove `card_number` and `cvc` from the transaction; they must not be persisted. Remove the `console.log`.

findings.title: Password hash returned in the register API response
findings.message: The `/auth/register` response includes `password_hash: user.password_hash`. Even a weak hash should never be sent to the client. Remove `password_hash` from the response object.

findings.title: Password reset token returned in the HTTP response body
findings.message: The reset token is returned in the API response (`res.json({ ok: true, resetToken })`), which means it can be logged by reverse proxies, CDNs, and monitoring tools. Password reset tokens must be delivered exclusively via email (or another out-of-band channel) to the address being reset. Remove `resetToken` from the response.

findings.title: `TransactionRepository.findAll()` does not exist — admin endpoints crash at runtime
findings.message: `AdminService` calls `this.transactions.findAll()` in `listAllTransactions`, `stats`, and `exportTransactionsCsv`, but the `TransactionRepository` interface (and its `InMemoryTransactionRepository` implementation) only defines `save` and `findById`. `@ts-nocheck` at the top of the file silences the TypeScript error. All three methods will throw `TypeError: this.transactions.findAll is not a function` at runtime. Add `findAll(): Promise<Transaction[]>` to the interface and implement it in the repository.

findings.title: JWTs are signed with no expiry — tokens are valid forever
findings.message: `jwt.sign(payload, SECRET)` is called without an `expiresIn` option. Tokens never expire, so a leaked or stolen token grants permanent access with no way to invalidate it short of rotating the secret for every user. Add `{ expiresIn: '15m' }` (or a similar short value) and implement a refresh-token flow.

findings.title: Login returns 404 for unknown email — enables user enumeration
findings.message: Returning a distinct `404 not_found` response for "no user with that email" allows attackers to enumerate valid email addresses. Return `401 invalid_credentials` with the same generic message for both wrong email and wrong password.

findings.title: `cors({ origin: '*', credentials: true })` is invalid and misconfigured
findings.message: The CORS spec disallows the wildcard origin when credentials are included — browsers will reject the response. More importantly, `origin: '*'` would allow any website to call the API. Specify an explicit allowlist of trusted origins (e.g., your frontend domain) and remove `credentials: true` unless cookies are actually required.

findings.title: Module-level `webhooks` array fires all owners' hooks for every transaction
findings.message: `fireForTransaction` iterates over the entire module-level `webhooks` array (line 33), not just hooks belonging to the transaction's owner. This means user A's webhook URL receives notifications for user B's transactions. Filter by the transaction's `customerId` / owner. The same module-level singleton also causes cross-test state pollution when multiple `createApp()` calls are made in tests.

findings.title: `requireAdmin` middleware defined but never used; admin check duplicated 4 times
findings.message: `requireAdmin` was added to `authMiddleware.ts` but the routes wire up `authMiddleware` only, and each of the four `AdminController` methods re-implements the same email-domain check inline. Use `requireAdmin` as a second route middleware (e.g., `router.get('/admin/users', authMiddleware, requireAdmin, admin.listUsers)`) and delete the four duplicated inline checks.

findings.title: `console.log` dumps the full request body including plaintext passwords
findings.message: `console.log('register request:', JSON.stringify(req.body))` logs the raw request body, which contains the user's plaintext password. The codebase uses pino with redaction; use `logger.debug({ email: req.body.email }, 'register_request')` instead and never log passwords.

findings.title: `console.log` leaks the raw SQL (including email) instead of using pino
findings.message: The entire codebase uses the pino logger. `console.log('userStore.findUserByEmail sql:', sql)` emits unstructured output that won't be filtered by log level or redacted. Replace with `logger.debug` or remove.

findings.title: Multiple `console.log` calls instead of project's pino logger
findings.message: `webhooksService.ts` has three `console.log` calls. One of them emits the full transaction payload (`payload:` on line 31), which — because the transaction now contains `card_number` and `cvc` — would log raw card data. Switch all to `logger.info` / `logger.warn` and do not log the full transaction payload.

findings.title: JSON body limit raised from 64 KB to 5 MB without justification
findings.message: A 5 MB JSON body limit on every endpoint (including public `/auth/register` and `/charge`) makes denial-of-service via large payloads cheap. The previous 64 KB was appropriate for a payments API. If the CSV export endpoint needs to return large responses, that is a response concern, not a request concern. Restore `64kb` or apply a larger limit only to the export route.

findings.title: `@ts-nocheck` suppresses real type errors that cause runtime crashes
findings.message: `// @ts-nocheck` at the top of `userStore.ts` (and `adminService.ts`) silences TypeScript entirely for these files. At least one of the suppressed errors (`findAll` not on `TransactionRepository`) is a genuine runtime crash. Remove `@ts-nocheck`, fix the type errors properly, and keep TypeScript's safety guarantees intact.

findings.title: Password reset flow has no tests and the token is not stored anywhere
findings.message: `generateResetToken` creates a token but it is never persisted (no store, no expiry, no lookup). The `/auth/password-reset` endpoint currently has no way to actually reset a password — there is no complementary `POST /auth/password-reset/confirm` route. The auth test suite covers only register and login. Add tests for the reset flow and implement token storage/validation.
